coredata.dc:title: Contribution of oligomer/carbon dots hybrid semiconductor nanoribbon to surface-enhanced Raman scattering property
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guiyang
Hu, Lin
Zhu, Kerong
Yan, Manqing
Liu, Jian
Yang, Jiaxiang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: The hybrid Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots nanobelts (NBs) have been prepared by decorating Ag nanoparticles (NPs) on surface of the ultra-long, semiconducting (PS-PSS)/C-dots nanoribbons (NRs) via an electroless plating method. The as-prepared Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB has been demonstrated to be an excellent substrate for surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) with a detection limit of 10−14 M and an enhancement factor of 3.35×108 while using rhodamine 6G as probe molecules. Moreover, we have investigated the application of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs as SERS substrate for detection of coumarins. Further, the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB could be used as a sacrificial template to form a novel kind of hollow porous Ag nanotubes (NTs) by simply removing the inner NR in tetrahydrofuran. However, the obtained Ag NTs show a weaker SERS effect compared to that of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs, which indicates that the inner organic/C-dots NR plays an essential role in SERS property of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs. Here the organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR not only acts as a dielectric support for Ag NPs to reduce the surface plasmon damping at the Ag-NR interface due to the high electrical conductivity but also their large surface area are favorable for creating more “hot-spots”. In addition, the embedded sp2-hybridized C-dots in NR can adsorb more aromatic R6G molecules via π–π interaction, which also drives R6G molecules approaching to the “hot-spots”, thus enhancing the SERS signals. Based on our results, it is believed that the employment of semiconducting organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots ribbon-like structures to fabricate sensitive SERS substrates is an interesting new approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Semiconductor
Carbon dots
Nanostructure
Surface-enhanced
Raman scattering (SERS)
Rhodamine 6G

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface-Decoration of Ag NPs on (PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
Preparation of Hollow Porous Ag NTs
Morphology and Structure of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
SERS Sensitivity of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
SERS EF of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs and detection limit of R6G
The SERS enhancement mechanism of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financed by the 211 Project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 51272002), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1208085ME87), and the Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar, Ministry of Personnel of China (No. [2013]-385). Colleges and universities in Anhui province, provincial outstanding young talent foundation project (Grant No. 2013SQRL066ZD). All co-authors thank the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Modern Bio-manufacture, Anhui University. We all also thank Prof. Tian Mingliang and Dr. Han Yuyan (the High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, China) for the measurement of I – V characteristics and Prof. Yang liangbao (Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, China) for his helpful discussion. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the fabrication process of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs and hollow porous Ag NTs for SERS.

(a) SEM and (b) TEM images of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs. (c) SEM image of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs. (d) A magnified TEM image of an individual Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB (the inset shows the gaps between neighbored nanoparticles are 5.54, 8.61 and 9.86nm, respectively). (e) TEM image of an individual Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB (the inset shows the corresponding SAED pattern). (f) XRD pattern of the Ag–(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs.

(a, b, c, d) TEM images of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-1min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-10min and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs, respectively. (e, f, g, h) Size distribution histograms of the Ag NPs in the corresponding NBs of a, b, c and d, respectively.

(a)785nm excited Raman spectra of 10−3 M R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-1min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-10min and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs, respectively. (b) 785nm excited Raman spectra of 10−2 M R6G in ethanol solution, 10−2 M R6G adsorbed on the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs and 10−2 M R6G adsorbed on the isolated Ag NPs in the same size.

(a) 785nm excited Raman spectra of (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB and 10−5 M R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min, respectively. (b) 785nm excited Raman spectra of R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB soaked in R6G/ethanol solutions with different concentrations (the inset is a magnified Raman spectrum of 10−14 M).

(a) UV–Vis spectra of a 10−5 M R6G solution and the supernatant of the R6G solution after it adsorbed by 0.3mg/mL Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs and Ag NPs-R6G, respectively. (b) UV–Vis spectra of a 10−5 M R6G solution, and the supernatant of the R6G solution after it adsorbed by 0.3mg/mL Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-1min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-10min, and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs, respectively.

(A,B) SEM images of the hollow porous Ag NTs at low and high magnifications, respectively. (C) A broken Ag NT. (D) Size distribution histogram of Ag NP aggregates in the hollow porous Ag NTs. (E,F) SEM images of hollow porous Ag NTs obtained after the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs dissolved in THF, shown at different magnifications.

785 nm-excited Raman spectra of (a) Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots, 10−4 M R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs, and 10−4 M R6G adsorbed on hollow porous Ag NTs, respectively; (b) different concentrations of R6G molecules adsorbed on hollow porous Ag NTs in ethanol solutions.

785nm excited Raman spectra of coumarins adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB soaked in coumarins/ethanol solutions with different concentrations. The concentrations of coumarins vary from up to bottom as follows: 10−8, 10−10, 10−12 and 10−14 M (the inset is a magnified Raman spectrum of 10−14 M).

head.ce:title: Contribution of oligomer/carbon dots hybrid semiconductor nanoribbon to surface-enhanced Raman scattering property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: See DOI: 10.1039/b000000x/.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hybrid Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots nanobelts (NBs) have been prepared by decorating Ag nanoparticles (NPs) on surface of the ultra-long, semiconducting (PS-PSS)/C-dots nanoribbons (NRs) via an electroless plating method. The as-prepared Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB has been demonstrated to be an excellent substrate for surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) with a detection limit of 10−14 M and an enhancement factor of 3.35×108 while using rhodamine 6G as probe molecules. Moreover, we have investigated the application of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs as SERS substrate for detection of coumarins. Further, the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB could be used as a sacrificial template to form a novel kind of hollow porous Ag nanotubes (NTs) by simply removing the inner NR in tetrahydrofuran. However, the obtained Ag NTs show a weaker SERS effect compared to that of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs, which indicates that the inner organic/C-dots NR plays an essential role in SERS property of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs. Here the organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR not only acts as a dielectric support for Ag NPs to reduce the surface plasmon damping at the Ag-NR interface due to the high electrical conductivity but also their large surface area are favorable for creating more “hot-spots”. In addition, the embedded sp2-hybridized C-dots in NR can adsorb more aromatic R6G molecules via π–π interaction, which also drives R6G molecules approaching to the “hot-spots”, thus enhancing the SERS signals. Based on our results, it is believed that the employment of semiconducting organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots ribbon-like structures to fabricate sensitive SERS substrates is an interesting new approach.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there is renewed interest in both the fundamentals and applications of surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) although it was discovered several decades ago [1]. SERS offers a tremendous enhancement of the intensity of the vibrational signals over 10 orders of magnitude, which has opened a new avenue for conventional Raman spectroscopy and made it a very useful tool for chemical and biomolecular detection [2,3]. Typically, noble metals, particularly such as gold (Au) and silver (Ag), have shown excellent SERS sensitivity and thus been called SERS-active materials. It is widely accepted that electromagnetic field (EM) enhancement and chemical effect are responsible for the SERS activity [4,5]. The former is associated with the localized surface plasmon resonances (SPR) occurring at the surfaces of rough metal substrates, which leads to more intense Raman scattering from molecules near or adsorbed onto the metal surface, and the latter involves specific interactions between analyte molecules and the metal surface, including the direct charge transfer or indirect electron-hole pair excitation processes [6,7]. A wide range of strategies, particularly new concepts on design of SERS substrates have been developed by use of assembled or aligned nanowire (NW) arrays [8,9], or high-quality 2D crystals decorated with Au nanorods [10] for enhancement of the SERS property. New techniques for nanofabrication, the design of substrates that maximize the EM enhancement, and the discovery of single-molecule SERS are driving the resurgence of SERS studies [1,11].

Among all of the SERS-active substrates, Ag shows a superior SERS performance and various Ag nanostructures with the uniform and highly reproducible SERS-active properties have been obtained, such as Ag nanoparticles (NPs) array [12], Ag NWs [13], Ag nanorods array [14], hierarchical peony-like Ag microflowers [15], Ag nanoplates [16] or hexagonal nanoplates embedded in the alumina gel-like porous matrix [17], and “flower-like” Ag NPs [18]. More experiments have proven that the extremely high SERS enhancement factors (EFs) and single-molecule sensitivity come from the compact, non-fractal Ag particle cluster, dimer or larger because the EM field in the gap region of them can be drastically amplified [3,19]. It is also demonstrated that the EFs of multiparticle aggregates for Ag would be several orders of magnitude higher than individual NPs due to the collective SPRs with interparticle plasmon coupling [19,20]. The enhancement can be further enlarged by constructing NPs very close to each other since the EM enhancement is highly sensitive to the shape of NPs and the interparticle distance [21]. Notably, a few reports revealed that high SERS sensitivity came from honeycomb architecture of carbon quantum dots (C-dots) [22] and graphene-quantum-dots assembled nanotubes (GQD-NTs) [23] which was probably associated with the collective properties of ensembles. In particular, silicon (Si) NWs coated with Ag NPs have been found favorable for magnifying SERS signals since the large surface area of the Si NWs is available to create more “hot spots” [24,25]. Recently, it was demonstrated that the SERS signal intensity can be enhanced by increasing the aspect ratio of the Si NWs when their height is less than 1μm [26].

On the other hand, if an Ag NP placed near a dielectric substrate where no plasmons exist, the charges induced are weak. This weak interaction can be sufficient to break the symmetry of the dielectric environment of the NP, lifting the degeneracy between its plasmon modes [2]. Recently, the Ag/PVA nanofiber mat [3], electrospun nanofibrous polyacrylonitrile membranes decorated with Ag NPs [27], and electrospun TiO2/Ag nanofelts [28] have been reported to be promising as SERS-active substrates for cost-effective and reproducible applications. Compared to Ag/polymer nanocomposites, Ag-graphene composite nanosheets [29] may be more appropriate SERS-active substrates because of a great SPR-assisted EM enhancement apart from a possible charge transfer between the analyte molecules and the graphene substrates, which result in a chemical enhancement. Indeed, it has been observed that the intensities of the Raman signals on monolayer graphene are much stronger than that on a SiO2/Si substrate [30]. However, when graphene is used as a substrate to support metals, the damping of plasmons on the graphene layer reduces the localized light intensity. A thin dielectric layer, i.e. HfO2 between the graphene layer and Au NPs could reduce the surface plasmon damping at the Au–graphene interface, and thus the Raman intensity increases sharply by plasmonic resonance enhancement while maintaining efficient fluorescence quenching [31]. Very recently, it was reported that the EM enhancement could be readily tuned by the number of graphene layers sandwiched between Au nanoparticles, which demonstrated a high EF of 2.5×108 for detecting rhodamine 6G (R6G). [21] Nevertheless, the EFs of aforementioned Ag NPs or alternative nanostructures, Ag/polymer nanocomposites and Ag-graphene nanosheets are far away from the ideal theoretical EF value of 1014–1015 [25].

To date, the main challenge is how to easily prepare SERS-active substrates with that can provide great detection sensitivity. In our previous work, a kind of ultra-long and semiconducting nanoribbons (NRs) have been synthesized by C-dots induced self-assembly of oligomers of styrene (St) and sodium 4-styrenesulfonate (NaSS) and thus named as (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs [32]. Here we demonstrate that the hybrid Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots nanobelts (NBs) can be prepared by decorating (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs surface with Ag NPs via a facile electroless plating method as illustrated in Fig. 1 . As a control, the hollow porous Ag nanotubes (NTs) were simply prepared by soaking the hybrid NBs in tetrahydrofuran (THF). However, the Ag NTs exhibit the weaker SERS signals compared to those of the hybrid NBs, indicating the significant role of SERS-active substrates. Although we were not the first to use of surface decorated fiber-like structures for SERS applications [33], the employment of semiconducting organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots ribbon-like structures is an interesting new approach. This new and important finding would be beneficial for the SERS community as well as for the material scientists.

St from Aldrich was passed through a basic alumina column, then vacuum distilled from calcium hydride (CaH2) and stored at −20°C prior to use. NaSS and potassium persulphate (KPS) purchased from Sinopharm were used as received. R6G was purchased from Aldrich. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) were purchased from Sinopharm (Shanghai, China) and used without further puriﬁcation.

The preparation of (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs is according to the previous report in our lab. [32] In a typical synthesis, 50mg of KPS and 6mg of NaSS were dissolved in ethanol/water mixture of (16.5ml/4.5ml) in a 25-mL three-neck flask equipped with a reflux condenser and a Teflon-coated magnetic stirring bar. This system was heated to 70°C under magnetic stirring. When the temperature reached 70°C, 0.2mL of a St was added to the flask, and 2min later, 3.0mL of the C-dots solution in ethanol (0.15mg/mL) was added in dropwise. The reaction system was kept heating at 70°C for another 2h before the product was collected. The product was obtained by precipitation in cold diethyl ether twice, centrifugation, and then washing with a mixture of ethanol/water (4/1, v/v) three times as well as ethanol one time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface-Decoration of Ag NPs on (PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
ce:section.ce:para: Surface-decoration of Ag NPs was carried out via an electroless plating method similar to the literature [27]. Firstly, 15M NH4OH solution was added into 1mL of 0.04M AgNO3 solution dropwise until the brown precipitate dissolved under the stirring, and then 15wt% NaOH solution was added into the system, resulting in the formation of brown precipitate; thereafter, additional 15M NH4OH solution was added in dropwise until the system became clear and colorless again. Secondly, the Tollen's reagent was prepared by mixing the Ag-precursor with 1mL 0.011M dextrose aqueous solution for 10s under stirring. Thirdly, (PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs were immersed in the Tollen's reagent for electroless plating of Ag with reaction time of 1, 3, 10 and 20min, respectively. Finally, the resulting Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB products were rinsed with a mixture of ethanol and water (4/1, v/v) for several times and then dried in air naturally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Hollow Porous Ag NTs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the Tollen's reagent was prepared by using 0.4mol of AgNO3. Secondly, (PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs was then immersed in the Tollen's reagent solution for electroless plating of Ag with reaction time of 5min. Thirdly, 2mL of THF was added to the reaction solution dropwise, and then the solution was stirred at room temperature for 30min. Finally, the hollow porous Ag NTs were collected after repeatedly washing with deionized water and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 1min for several times.

The morphologies of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB products were observed by using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (S-4800, Hitachi Co., Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a spectrometer (NEXUS-870, Nicolet Instrument Co., USA) in the range of 400–4000cm−1. The UV–Vis spectra of samples in aqueous solution were measured on a spectrophotometer (U-4100, Hitachi Co., Japan) in the range of 200–900nm. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on a XD-3 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The electrical characteristics of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB were carried out with table-top cryogenic probe station (Lake Shore, Model TTPX) using a Keithley 2400 sourcemeter.

The sample preparation procedure for Raman investigation is similar to that in literature [28], as illustrated in Fig. S1, ESI†. At first, predetermined amounts of R6G were dissolved in ethanol to make the R6G solutions with different concentrations. Second, the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB samples were soaked in the R6G solution at a certain concentration and then dried by a stream of N2 gas. One droplet of the corresponding R6G solution was also dropped on to the glass substrate and used as a reference for the Raman characterizations after drying. At last, SERS spectra were obtained by using a Renishaw Raman inVia Reflex spectroscopy system with a 532-nm or 785-nm laser. Both laser powers on samples were 0.5mW to avoid sample heating and photo-induced damages. The laser was focused onto the sample surface using a 50×long working distance objective. All of SERS spectra were collected at room temperature (ca. 20°C) in continuous mode with an acquisition time of 30s and accumulated for 1 time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and Structure of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2a and b shows SEM and TEM images of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs, respectively. The structure and formation mechanism of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR have been described in our previous report [32], which is built on electrostatic self-assembly of alternating C-dots and PS-PSS oligomer blocks while the C-dots stacking along length direction with intermediate polymer layers. The obtained NRs are very wide and thin with a width of 1-2 μm and an ultra-long length (>100μm). Upon reaction with the Tollen's reagent, [Ag(NH3)2]+ ions were reduced into Ag atoms as shown in Eq. (1) [27,34]:
(1) D-CHO+2[Ag(NH3)2]+ +3OH− →2Ag+D-COO -
+4NH3 +2H2O

The generated Ag atoms would grow into NPs on the surface of NR during the electroless plating process. Fig. 2c shows a typical SEM image of the as-prepared Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs with the reaction time of 3min, in which the surface is rough and decorated with a plenty of tiny Ag NPs. A magnified TEM image of an individual NB in Fig. 2d reveals that the Ag NPs in average size of 10–20nm distribute homogeneously on the surface of the NB and the gaps between neighbored nanoparticles are 5.54, 8.61 and 9.86nm, respectively. The corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the NB is in concentric rings (the inset of Fig. 2e), which indicates that the polycrystalline Ag NPs are randomly attached to the surface of NBs without particular orientations. Moreover, Fig. 2f displays the XRD pattern of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs, confirming the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure of Ag NPs in the NB. There are four peaks at 2θ=38.2, 44.4, 64.6, and 77.4, assigning to (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of fcc-Ag, respectively. The mean grain size can be estimated from the half-width of the XRD peaks according to Scherrer’ formula [35]. As a result, the calculated grain size of Ag NPs is 17.8nm, which is in agreement with the average particle size acquired from TEM analyses of Fig. 2d. It is found that the size and quantity of Ag NPs mainly depend on reaction time. The aggregation size of Ag NPs can be tuned by controlling the reaction time of electroless plating. Fig. 3 shows TEM images and the corresponding size distribution histograms of Ag NPs in four NB samples that have reacted for 1, 3, 10 and 20min, respectively. Obviously, the mean size of Ag NPs increased from 5.9nm to 14.1, 42.4 and 55. 8nm as the reaction time prolongs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS Sensitivity of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation size of Ag NPs will play an important role in determining the SERS sensitivity of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs. Since the degree of NP aggregation is the key to the enhancement, controlling the aggregation process could lead to optimal SERS detection [36]. Fig. 4a shows SERS sensitivities of 10−3 M R6G adsorbed on the surface of above four hybrid NB samples. Notably, the strongest Raman signal was collected from the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min sample that with a small but not the least mean size of Ag NPs. In addition, here the 785nm excitation light was used because of the following four reasons: (1) can avoid fluorescence background of organic dye molecules and photochemical degradation or transformation of adsorbed species [17]; (2) longer wavelength excitation, i.e. 785nm is less harmful to the biological samples; (3) also can avoid the interference of strong fluorescence of C-dots under 532nm excitation; (4) above all, R6G does not present Raman resonances at this wavelength. This is a very important point even though it is still not fully clear to what extent the Raman resonance of a molecule is quenched by the surface plasmons. Anyway, in many published articles that claim to have achieved an extraordinary SERS enhancement using R6G as a probe molecule, the illumination wavelength used is resonant and, thus, the enhancement is not solely due to surface effects but also to Raman resonance effects (maybe quenched to some extent but not completely suppress), i.e., it is not SERS but SERRS (Surface Enhanced Resonant Raman Scattering). In the present case, only pre-resonance effects could contribute to the Raman signal. As shown in Fig. 4b, We have collected 785 nm-excited Raman signals of 10−2 M R6G dropping on to the glass substrate and used as a reference for the Raman characterizations after drying, 10−2 M R6G dropping on the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs and 10−2 M R6G dropping on the isolated Ag NPs obtained by dissolving the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs in THF. In contrast, the Raman peak of 10−2 M R6G dropping onto the glass substrate was comparatively weak.

Furthermore, 532nm-excited Raman spectra of C-dots, (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs, and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs are also presented in Fig. S2, ESI†. As expected, the C-dots do not show any Raman signal due to interference of their fluorescence emission. So the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs do not show obvious Raman signal, either. However, the Raman spectrum of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots exhibits two prominent bands at 1333cm−1 and 1593cm−1, which is similar to those of the C-dots under the 325nm excitation, attributed to D and G bands of graphitic carbon atoms, respectively [32,37]. The intensity of D-band and G-band of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots composite is obviously higher than that of pure C-dots, which is due to the surface enhancement Raman scattering effect of AgNPs [38,39]. This result also indicates that there is photo-induced electron transfer from C-dots to Ag NPs, and thus fluorescence quenching happens to the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs after surface-decoration of Ag NPs, as shown in Fig. S3 (A, B), ESI†.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS EF of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs and detection limit of R6G
ce:section.ce:para: R6G was chosen as the model molecule since it is one of the most commonly used model molecules in SERS due to its large Raman scattering cross section and well-established Raman spectral information. [40] In the present case, as shown in Fig. S1 of ESI†, R6G molecules were adsorbed onto surface of the samples by soaking the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB samples in the R6G/ethanol solution at a certain concentration. Fig. 5a shows the Raman spectra of (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB and 10−5 M R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB, respectively. Table S1 in ESI† gives the 785nm Raman band assignments of R6G. As expected, prominent SERS signal with good signal-to-noise ratio is observed for the R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB, whereas R6G on the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR only shows typical benzene ring-related Raman peaks. Further, the 785 nm-excited Raman signals of R6G adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB in solutions of different concentrations were collected, as shown in Fig. 5b. The lowest concentration of R6G to be detected, namely, the detection limit of R6G reaches 10−14 M, which is even lower than the previously reported limit of 10−12 M of R6G on pure Ag NPs [41] or high-density Ag NP film [42].

To estimate the SERS enhancement, we calculated the Raman EF per molecule as the ratio of the intensity in presence or absence of the active substrate [17]. EF for the 1507cm−1 SERS band was estimated using the Eq. (2) [43,44]:
(2)
EF =
I
sers
/
I
ref
×
N
ref
/
N
sers

Where I sers is the intensity of the specific Raman band from the analyte adsorbed on a SERS-active substrate, and N sers is the number of molecules contributing to I sers. Similarly, I ref is the intensity of the same Raman band from the bulk analyte, normalized with the laser power and acquisition time, and N ref is the number of molecules that yield I ref. See also in detailed calculation of EFs, ESI†. Although Etchegoin and coworkers have been nicely addressed the calculation of EF and quantification of the SERS enhancement several years ago [44], it is a never-ending story of EF correct calculation to allow for an accurate quantification of the SERS magnitude [43,45]. Herein, the calculated EF for the 1507cm−1 SERS band of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB is about 3.35×108 that is greater than the previously reported substrates such as Ag NP arrays,12 Ag/polyacrylonitrile nanocomposites [27], Au-deposited C-dots honeycomb [22], (GQD-NTs)/Au [23], and Au@C-dots composite NPs [38]. It is worthwhile pointing out that in our calculation the R6G molecules are assumed to be fully adsorbed as a monolayer on the surface of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs. This hypothesis represents a theoretical maximum number of R6G molecules adsorbed on the surface, which is an overestimate of the actual EF value [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SERS enhancement mechanism of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs
ce:section.ce:para: As is well-known, many factors simultaneously contribute to the Raman enhancement effect. The fact has been proven countless times during the past years that the SERS magnitude depends on the size, size distribution, surface state, surrounding environment, shape, and nature of metal NPs [17,25,46]. However, it has been widely accepted that EM enhancement is the largest factor for the SERS effect [47]. As for the metal hybrid substrates, the Raman sensitivity can be enhanced by 108 or more. Most of enhanced SERS sensitivity has benefited greatly from SPR-assisted EM enhancement, where Raman scattering of molecules is enhanced by many orders of magnitude when the molecules are within the localized region with greatly amplified electric fields, or so-called “hot spots” [21,48]. The “hot spots” exist between adjacent nanoparticles and near the sharp corners and asperities of the metal nanostructure [13,48]. In the present case, the ultra-long hybrid NR has a very large surface area available for deposition of a plenty of Ag NPs. These Ag NPs would aggregate into clusters, dimers or the larger assemblies in a size of 10–20nm, in which the distance between adjacent Ag NPs decreases to an appropriate gap for the formation of more “hot spots”. In fact, tunneling effects between particles can reduce the local EM enhancement if the gaps between nanoparticles are below 1nm [11,49]. In the meanwhile, such rough surface of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB is more favorable for SPR-assisted EM enhancement when excited by those used in cited references [23,50–52]. More recent reports have confirmed that SERS signals mainly come from the “hot spots” which are formed at the gap region between two Ag NPs or the sharp surface of nanostructures [53–57]. Further, Fig. S4 in ESI† shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs and Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs in ethanol. Apart from the absorption band at 250–280nm of (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR, a broad tailband of reagent solution among the 250–700nm also appears in the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NBs (blue arrows representing area), having surface plasmon resonance (SPR) bands at a desired wavelength [56,58,59].

Besides EM enhancement from the more generated “hot spots”, the chemical interaction between Ag NPs and (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR also contributes to the enhanced Raman signals. As previously reported, [32] FT-IR spectrum of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR exhibiting a strong SO3 − absorption feature at 1081cm−1 owing to the PSS segments in the NR. However, the IR absorption of SO3 − group shifted remarkably to 1108cm−1 after the surface-decoration of Ag NPs on NR as shown in Fig. S5 of ESI†, possibly because the negatively charged SO3 − groups on the surface of NRs acted as active sites for adsorption of Ag+ cations during the electroless plating process. Moreover, the aforementioned fluorescence quenching in the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs indicates that there is photo-induced electron transfer from C-dots to Ag NPs, in the meanwhile, the sp2-hybridized C-dots in NR can adsorb more aromatic R6G molecules via π–π interaction. This chemical interaction between R6G and the C-dots can drive R6G molecules approaching to the “hot-spots”, thus enhancing the probability of a correct placement of the R6G molecules on the active SERS sites. Fig. 6 shows much more R6G molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs than those onto the isolated Ag NPs in the same size (these isolated Ag NPs were obtained by dissolving the hybrid Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs in THF for long time enough). Similarly, Au@C-dots hybrid NPs exhibited SERS performance superior to that of pure Au NPs, which was mainly caused by Au@C-dots absorbing more aromatic dye molecules via π–π stacking and electrostatic interaction. [38] Although there are many manifestations of chemical interaction at interfaces, our understanding of these phenomena is limited. [57] Further works are required to develop a comprehensive understanding for this chemical interaction SERS.

Furthermore, the embedding dielectric medium can also play a vital role. It is known that when metal NPs are embedded in a high dielectric medium, alteration of SPR with significant enhancement of intensity is possible, which can contribute towards high Raman enhancement. [17,60–63] In the present case, the dielectric matrix of Ag NPs is the semiconducting (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR that is composed of alternating C-dots and organic PS-PSS oligomer blocks. [32] Moreover, the dielectric PS-PSS oligomer blocks within the NR could reduce the surface plasmon damping at the Ag NP-NR interface. In addition, Fig. S6 of ESI† indicates that the electric conductivity of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR was estimated to be 3.368S/m while that of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB increases 2 order of magnitude to 3.368×102 S/m. The nonlinear I–V curves of NR reveals the semiconducting behavior of the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs while the linear I–V curves and much higher conductivity of NB demonstrate that the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs are conductive after surface decoration of Ag NPs. As a control, the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs were immersed in THF for enough time so that the inner (PS-PSS)/C-dots NRs was removed and then a novel kind of hollow porous Ag NTs could be obtained. Fig. 7A and B shows SEM images of the hollow porous Ag NTs at low and high magnifications, respectively. Some NTs were broken during the preparation as shown in Fig. 7C, E and F, since the process was a fast self-sacrificial dissolved process of the NRs. Nevertheless, it is noticed that the hollow porous Ag NTs in Fig. 7 have very large intermediate space, the possible reason is that such a wide, thin and soft organic (PS-PSS)/C dots ribbon would roll into hollow fiber-like structure during the process of chemical deposition of Ag NPs on its surface, therefore, the hollow porous Ag NTs look like prepared by removing fibers but not thin belts.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 8a, the Raman signals of R6G on hollow porous Ag NTs were also collected and compared with that on the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs, since these two systems have nearly equivalent mean size of Ag NPs of about 55nm and 65nm, respectively. However, Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs have obviously larger inter-particle distances between Ag NPs than that in hollow porous Ag NTs. Apparently, the hollow porous Ag NTs present weaker Raman signal enhancement than that of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-20min NBs. Fig. 8b shows the concentration-dependent SERS spectra of R6G molecules adsorbed on hollow porous Ag NTs in ethanol solutions. In this case, the detection limit of R6G is 10−10 M that is greatly lower than that the semiconducting organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots substrate. The main reason is that higher coalescence of the Ag NPs in the case of the NTs resulting in a lower density of “hot-spots”; that is to say, removal of the inner (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR substrate makes the Ag NPs towards more aggregation. This result confirms the semiconducting organic (PS-PSS)/C-dots substrate plays an important role in SERS property of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs. As above-mentioned, when metal NPs are embedded in a high dielectric medium, alteration of SPR with significant enhancement of intensity is possible, which can contribute towards high Raman enhancement. Here the (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR not only acts as a dielectric support for Ag NPs to reduce the surface plasmon damping at the Ag-NR interface and create more “hot-spots”, but also can adsorb more R6G molecules via π–π interaction between the embedded sp2-hybridized C-dots in NR and aromatic rings of R6G. This chemical interaction between R6G and the C-dots also drives R6G molecules approaching to the “hot-spots”, thus enhancing the probability of a correct placement of the R6G molecules on the active SERS sites. Despite the tremendous efforts and major advances that have been achieved in SERS research, the exact mechanisms of SERS are still a matter of debate. [64]

The above-mentioned large enhancement suggests that the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs can indeed serve as robust substrates for SERS. Furthermore, we have investigated the application of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs as SERS substrate for detection of coumarins (2H-1-benzopyran-2-ones). [65] Coumarins are well-known fluorescent organic dyes with a broad range of biological activities. The 785 nm-excited Raman signals of coumarins adsorbed on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots-3min NB in solutions of different concentrations were collected, as shown in Fig. 9 . The Raman spectra confirm the superior Raman enhancement on Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB substrate as the detection limit reaches as low as 10−12 M of coumarins. This result demonstrate the potential of Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NBs for more extensive SERS applications. We expect this highly sensitive and the hybrid Ag NB-based SERS platform may serve as a practical and powerful tool for biomolecular sensing and biomedical analysis, i.e. DNA detection.

We have successfully prepared a sensitive SERS substrate by an electroless plating of Ag NPs on surface of an ultra-long and wide, semiconducting (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR by self-assembly of C-dots and PS-PSS oligomers on the basis of electrostatic interactions. The detection limits for coumarins or R6G molecules reach as low as 10−14 M. It is found that the semiconducting (PS-PSS)/C-dots NR plays an essential role in SERS of the Ag-(PS-PSS)/C-dots NB. In the future, the search for SERS-active nanostructured substrates with enormous enhancement will continue to be a frontier in the field.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the 211 Project of Anhui University, the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 51272002), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (1208085ME87), and the Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar, Ministry of Personnel of China (No. [2013]-385). Colleges and universities in Anhui province, provincial outstanding young talent foundation project (Grant No. 2013SQRL066ZD). All co-authors thank the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, and Collaborative Innovation Center of Modern Bio-manufacture, Anhui University. We all also thank Prof. Tian Mingliang and Dr. Han Yuyan (the High Magnetic Field Laboratory, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, China) for the measurement of I–V characteristics and Prof. Yang liangbao (Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, China) for his helpful discussion.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2015.12.214.
